title: Willa Magland’s…
text:
Hello! 👋
My name is Willa, and welcome to my teeny tiny guide for using the internet! If you haven’t noticed, the web has gone wayyyy downhill recently. You’re probably used to being bombarded with personalized ads, endless algorithms, AI slop, and other nasty stuff 🦠
The good news is, there is a better way to use the internet! A way that makes it private, secure, and fun, all at the same time!
I’m way tired of watching the people I love having negative experiences online, so I made this guide to offer some help. Here, I’ll explain which tools I use (and trust) to keep my web-browsing quick, clean, and private. I’ve tried to be detailed, yet concise, so I hope you will give it a chance!






Tip



In a rush? Check out the starter pack for my recommended setup!








Note



To go to the next page, click the “Background” button at the bottom. You can view all the pages via the menu on the left!

title: Search Engines
text:
Note



DuckDuckGo and Startpage are pretty much equally as private, so choose whichever one you like the best. I just happen to like DuckDuckGo, so I ranked it first.


While a web browser allows you to access things on the internet, a search engine helps you find the websites you’re looking for. Examples of search engines are Google, Yahoo, and my recommendation, DuckDuckGo.
Google has spent millions of dollars trying to make their search engine a go-to source for information. This is very dangerous, and cuts out the user’s ability to select reputable sources from the results. DuckDuckGo does what a search engine is supposed to do: it gives you a list of websites that match your search. From there, you can select the most trustworthy source that suits your needs
Like other search engines, it has some ads, but these are based on the content of your search and not your personal information. If you install the adblocker I recommend on the next page, uBlock Origin, you won’t see ads at all!
Another good alternative is Startpage, which shares a lot of great features with DuckDuckGo.




Link to website




DuckDuckGo 🦆
https://start.duckduckgo.com/


Startpage 🔎
https://www.startpage.com









Note



DuckDuckGo has an AI summary feature, but it can be turned off permenantly in just two clicks. It also has a mobile app. If you chose Librewolf as your browser, DuckDuckGo is already set as your default search engine.








Tip



If you chose Waterfox as your browser, Startpage is already set as your default search engine.

title: Background
text:
Before we start, it’s important to have a basic understanding of what makes a web browsing experience before we change anything.
In general, there are three main components that make up your web-browsing experience.

The browser (Chrome, Firefox, Opera…): the app that allows us to open and look at websites
The search engine (Google, DuckDuckGo, Yahoo…): websites which help us find other sites
Browser extensions: software that is added on top of the browser to change what it does

In the next three sections we’ll look at each of these components and choose the best possible options to get things safe and snappy. Each section will have an explanation of each component with links to my recommended apps and websites at the bottom.
OK let’s goooooo! 🚀

title: Conclusion
text:
You made it to the end! 🎉
Firstly, thank you for reading my guide! If any part was confusing, please let me know and I’ll update it. I hope it made your life a little better, and here’s to a fun, free, and private internet!

title: Browser Extensions
text:
Woo-hoo! Almost done!
Browser extensions are bits of software that you layer on top of your web browser to give it extra features. For Firefox and other Firefox-based browsers (like Librewolf and Waterfox), you can download them at https://addons.mozilla.org/. On Chrome and Chrome-based browsers, you can find them at the Chrome Web Store.
While the right extensions can make your web browsing more private, there are a lot of ugly ones out there. In general, it’s best to have only a few, vetted browser extensions to reduce risk and keep things snappy.

Adblocking 🚫






Note



Google has blocked UBlock Origin in Chrome, hence why I recommend using a Firefox based browser instead. To install it anyway, follow this guide.


Having a good adblocker installed is probably the biggest step you can take to improve your web browsing experience and privacy. Despite what Google tries to make you think, an adblocker is essential security software for the modern web. Not only does a good adblocker block ads, but hundreds of weird trackers that trace your activity. I recommend uBlock Origin, as it’s the current gold standard for browsing safely. It does all of the above, while also warning you about suspicious websites, offering protection from viruses and malware.




Link to website




uBlock Origin
https://ublockorigin.com/









Tip



If you’re using the Librewolf browser, it’s installed by default, so you don’t have to do anything!




Password Managers 🔒
Most likely the largest vulnerability in your web browsing right now is your passwords. You’re probably using similar passwords for each of your logins, which leaves you open to being “hacked,” but it’s not your fault!
Passwords are bad user design by nature. Luckily, there’s a solution that makes signing into things super fast, and it only requires memorizing one password: a password manager. It can autofill your login details almost instantaneously, and it also handles generating new, secure passwords for your new logins. Hell, it can even handle pesky 2-factor authentication codes! It does this all while being extremely secure, private, and convenient.

That sounds risky…
You might be feeling wary about giving a website all of your login information. But this is the zinger: by design, you don’t need to trust your password manager. Password managers use a special encryption technology that allows only you to access your passwords. That “one password” I mentioned earlier is called the master password, and is used to decrypt your login information, so in reality, the password manager can’t see a thing.
This video explains it better than I can, but back to the guide!




Link to website




Bitwarden (free)
https://bitwarden.com/


1Password
https://1password.com/









Tip



As long as you pick a generally well-known and reputable one, it doesn’t really matter which password manager you choose. I use (and pay for) Bitwarden, and my Dad enjoys 1Password, but there are other great ones out there!








Important



Please, next to installing uBlock Origin, if there is one thing you take away from this guide, it should be signing up for a password manager! It will change your life, I guarentee it. You don’t deserve having to remember a million passwords!





Mmmm… Cookies 🍪
So we’ve taken care of those annoying ads, made logging in a click away, but what about those annoying “accept cookies” popups? By law, websites need these to get your consent before collecting data about you, but they make it very hard to say no. Consent-O-Matic takes care of this for you by atomatically selecting the most privacy-preserving option each time. Goodbye, cookies!




Link to website




Consent-O-Matic
https://consentomatic.au.dk/

title: Browsers
text:
WarningBeware Brave!



Despite what online privacy guides recommend, DO NOT USE BRAVE! Brave is a private for-profit browser, and is notorious for a lot of gross cryptocurrency stuff. Also, the CEO sucks. Don’t use it! (More Info)
A web browser is the app you click on to access the internet. Its job is to fetch and render all of the websites you visit. Because of this, which browser you choose has a large impact on your web experience.

title: Browsers
text:
Firefox, Chrome, and “Forks” 🍴
There are only two mainstream browsers: Chrome and Firefox. Most other browsers (Opera, Edge, Librewolf) are just slight variations of either Chrome or Firefox. These variations are called “forks.”
However, these slight changes can mean a lot for privacy and web experience. In general, Firefox is usually faster and more private. However, generic Firefox still comes with some creepy code and a weird privacy policy, which is why I recommend using a Firefox fork such as Librewolf or Waterfox.

title: Browsers
text:
Waterfox 🌊 and Librewolf 🐺
Waterfox and Librewolf are open source, community maintained “forks” of Firefox. They come with better default privacy options, and get rid of all the unnecessary junk like AI and feeds.
If you’re not a very tech-inclined person, I recommend you go with Waterfox, which focuses on usability. If you’re more tech-minded and want to maximize privacy, I recommend Librewolf, which is what I use. Both Waterfox and Librewolf are great choices and are massive privacy upgrades over stock Firefox and Chrome.
If you must use a Chrome-based browser (I highly recommend you don’t!) I recommend ungoogled-chromium, which is basically Chrome but without all of Google’s icky stuff, plus extra privacy enhancements.




Link to website




Waterfox 🌊
https://www.waterfox.com/


Librewolf 🐺
https://librewolf.net/


Ungoogled Chromium1
https://ungoogled-software.github.io/ungoogled-chromium-binaries/









Tip



What about Opera? Opera is a chrome-based, closed-source, for-profit browser owned by a chinese company. Privacy-wise, it is not an upgrade over Chrome, so I don’t recommend it. It’s known for its free VPN, see my note on VPNS.








Note



Firefox is still a huge upgrade over Chrome, so if you’re deciding between the two, choose Firefox!


Now that you’ve got a sweet new browser, kit it out with a search engine! 🔎

title: A Note On VPNs
text: One of the biggest myths about privacy is that you need a VPN to be private. The truth is, you probably don’t, and here’s why.

title: A Note On VPNs
text:
What is a VPN?
VPN stands for Virtual Private Network. When you connect to a VPN, your internet traffic is routed through another server before reaching its destination. This way, it appears as though you’re accessing the internet from another location.
For example, if I connected to a VPN in Canada from the USA and tried to watch YouTube, my internet traffic would go from my computer, to a server in Canada, and then to YouTube, instead of directly from my computer to YouTube.
Because it appears as if I’m accessing YouTube from Canada, I might be able to watch YouTube videos that are blocked in the USA. Also, it hides my IP address, which can be used to tell my approximate location in the USA.

title: A Note On VPNs
section: Why you probably don’t need one
text:
Why you probably don’t need one
Unless you live in certain countries (and some US states), you probably have access to everything you need to. It’s also unlikely that you need to worry about hiding your IP address from the websites you visit, as IP addresses are generally pretty public bits of information.
Following this guide will do 10x more for your privacy than getting a VPN ever will, so don’t let NordVPN fool you into thinking you need one!
You should consider a VPN if you are…

a journalist
an activist
a privacy freak (looking at you, Jasmine)
a member of an international hacking group

…in THAT case, I recommend you use Mullvad VPN.




Link to website




Mullvad VPN
https://mullvad.net/en
